context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Course context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: uhh idk, PS4 is frying my brain rn 😭 can we do 4.1?
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to work through 4.1 with you! Let's start at the beginning.

Look at the left wall. Physically, what must be true about the displacement of the string at the wall?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting fresh on problem 4.1. I should orient them to the problem without giving anything away, and use a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the physics of the boundary condition at the left wall. I'll keep it brief and ask them what they know about the physical constraint the wall imposes on the string.

exchanges.student: i'm already kinda lost tbh, is the wall point just zero displacement or is it about the fake bead thing?
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — zero displacement at the wall is exactly the physical constraint. Now, the problem asks you to represent that wall using a fictitious bead A0 in an extended infinite system. If the wall must always have zero displacement, what relationship between A0 and A1 would guarantee that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some initial confusion but is actually touching on the key idea — zero displacement at the wall. I should affirm the physical intuition about zero displacement and then nudge them toward connecting that to the 'image bead' idea (A1 = -A0), without giving it away. One focused question to guide them.

exchanges.student: maybe they have to cancel so the wall midpoint is 0? so like A0 = -A1 i think?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! Can you explain in your own words *why* equal and opposite amplitudes at symmetric points guarantees zero displacement at the wall?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got the right answer (A0 = -A1, which is equivalent to A1 = -A0 as stated in the problem). I should confirm the physical reasoning is correct and push them to articulate WHY equal and opposite amplitudes at symmetric points guarantee zero displacement at the midpoint — this will solidify their understanding before moving to part (b).

exchanges.student: kinda?? i just know it cancels at the wall, but idk if i'm supposed to think symmetry/interpolation/string segment stuff or what fr
exchanges.tutor: Keep it simple — if the bead at position 0 has amplitude A0 and the bead at position 1 has amplitude A1 = −A0, what is the displacement exactly halfway between them (i.e., at the wall)?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track but is overthinking the explanation. I should guide them to think simply about what happens at the midpoint between A0 and A1 when they are equal and opposite — it's just superposition/averaging. A simple nudge without overloading them.

exchanges.student: uh halfway would be the average, so \((A0 + A1)/2 = 0\), right? i'm not sure if that's the whole justification tho
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid justification — the wall sits exactly at the midpoint, and with A1 = −A0 the displacement there is always zero, which matches the physical boundary condition. Good.

Now for part (b): the right wall has a similar setup, but where exactly is it located relative to the beads, and what does that tell you about the boundary condition there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right intuition and the averaging argument works as a justification for part (a). I should affirm this is sufficient and move them to part (b) to keep momentum going, since we only have a planned 10-exchange conversation.

exchanges.student: wait i’m mixed up again — is it between AN and a fake A_{N+1}, so then AN = -A_{N+1}?
exchanges.tutor: Check the figure — where exactly is the right wall relative to bead N? Is the geometry the same as the left side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right structure but I want them to verify the geometry themselves — the right wall is at distance a/2 from bead N, mirroring the left side. I should ask them to confirm this from the figure rather than just accepting their guess.

exchanges.student: yeah i think bead N is a/2 from the wall too, so it's the same idea but i keep second-guessing the indexing ngl
exchanges.tutor: You've got it right — the geometry is symmetric. So go ahead and write down the boundary condition for the right wall. Don't second-guess yourself!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right physical picture. I should affirm the geometry and nudge them to commit to the boundary condition in writing, pushing toward part (c) since we are progressing well.

exchanges.student: ok then i’d write \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\) i think, but tbh part (c) is where i fully black out 😵
exchanges.tutor: Correct! Now for part (c) — what do normal mode solutions look like for an infinite beaded string? What's the general form you'd write down for the amplitude of bead n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified both boundary conditions. I should affirm and transition to part (c). For part (c), I want to use Socratic questioning to guide them — starting with what they already know about normal modes in an infinite beaded string system, since the boundary conditions will then constrain the allowed modes.

exchanges.student: i forget the whole setup rn, is it like \(A_n \sim e^{ikna}\) or do i need sines/cosines from the start?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's the right starting point. Now apply your two boundary conditions (A1 = −A0 and A_{N+1} = −A_N) to that exponential form. What does the first one tell you about the allowed form of A_n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with the exponential form. I should affirm this is the right starting point and then guide them to apply the boundary conditions they just found to this general form, which will naturally lead to standing wave solutions (sines) and quantized k values.

exchanges.student: i’m not seeing it tbh, can u just explain the whole part c in one shot bc i can’t connect the steps
exchanges.tutor:
I can't walk you through the whole solution — that's for you to work out! But let's take it one step at a time.

Plug A_n = e^{ikna} into A1 = −A0. You get e^{ika} = −e^{0}. What does that tell you about k?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the full solution, which I must refuse. However, they seem genuinely stuck, so I should be less Socratic and more concrete by breaking it into one small step — applying just the left boundary condition to the exponential. I'll also remind them of my role. I should not give the answer but can offer a very targeted hint to get them unstuck on the first step.

deductions.reason: In Turn 2, when the student says they are 'already kinda lost,' the tutor immediately provides the answer ('zero displacement at the wall is exactly the physical constraint') and then moves to the next step without first diagnosing what specifically the student was confused about. The tutor jumps straight to addressing the issue rather than asking a diagnostic question to locate the confusion.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining a Socratic approach throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to give away answers and instead prompting the student with targeted sub-questions. The student is guided step by step through parts (a) and (b) of Problem 4.1, and when the student explicitly asks for the full solution to part (c), the tutor appropriately declines and breaks it into a single manageable step. The one notable weakness is in Turn 2, where the tutor confirms the correct physical constraint ('zero displacement') without first probing what specifically confused the student, missing a diagnostic opportunity.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions warranted. The tutor never provides submission-ready answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets the student generate key ideas before confirming them. 1.2: A 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a is applied for Turn 2, where the student signals confusion ('already kinda lost') and the tutor immediately validates the correct answer and advances rather than asking a diagnostic question to locate the confusion. 1.3: The tutor provides process-level feedback (e.g., 'Keep it simple,' 'Don't second-guess yourself,' affirming the student's reasoning method) sufficient to avoid deduction. 2.1 and 2.2: No repetition without progress and no off-topic drift observed. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses are concise, well-paced, and supportive throughout; no tone or grading issues.
